text:
Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly becoming part and parcel of everyday life. Despite the immense benefits that AI technology affords, there are numerous concerns raised as to its negative impact on fundamental rights and the public interest in case of the uncontrolled use of these technologies. As a reaction, many regulators around the globe have come up with a plethora of norms and guidelines, almost all non-binding in nature. The first real test of binding nature of the legislative framework will be provided by seeing how the new Artificial Intelligence Act (as adopted in spring 2024 by the European Union) functions in practice.

Coming into force formally on 1 August 2024, the new Act will establish a risk-based framework of rules applicable to all sectors. It will be the first, if not the only, regulation to take a systematic approach to the development and use of AI: High-risk uses of AI will have to be implemented in line with strict, legally binding, rules while banning uses considered to be at risk of undermining the protection of fundamental rights. With its extraterritorial scope and as a benchmark for comparable regulations in other parts of the world, the AI Act as it stands will be a unique, indeed unprecedented, innovation that will shape and affect the development and the use of AI in Europe as well as beyond.
